coredata.dc:title: Environmentally friendly gelatin-based ink with durability on low surface energy substrates for anti-counterfeiting printing
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wenshuo
Zhang, Tao
Deng, Yajun
Guan, Zhihao
Zhong, Yuye
Fang, Shuliang
Wang, Guannan
Liu, Xinghai
Li, Houbin
coredata.dc:description: Recent research is keen to introduce biomass materials into inks to develop "green” printing, but little attention has been given to whether these inks can be truly widely used in printing industry. There are few bio-based inks suitable for low surface energy plastic such as polyethylene glycol terephthalate (PET), which is one of the most common printing substrates. Herein, waterborne polyurethane with polyfluoroalkyl side chain (F-WPU) was designed and synthesized to prepare fluorocarbon quantum dots (F-CDs). F-WPU and F-CDs were mixed together with gelatin to obtain environmentally friendly inks which were utilized to print on a low surface energy substrate. F-WPU (30 wt%) and F-CDs (0.5 wt%) significantly improve the mechanical properties of composites by forming an interpenetrating network system and hydrogen bonds, respectively. Abundant carbon-fluorine bonds effectively reduce the surface energy of gelatin, resulting in excellent hydrophobicity and strong adhesion to the PET surface. The thermal stability of the composite could significantly enhance to meet the promising needs for the further engineering applications. Besides, F-CDs could emit blue-green fluorescence based on conjugated structure and π→π* transition under a wider excitation wavelength. Therefore, the complex exhibited good transparency under sunlight and performed significant cyan fluorescence under an ultraviolet irradiation, in which can be efficiently developed and used in anti-counterfeiting labels and printing. Furthermore, the friction, soaking and kneading tests have been measured to seriously take the considerations for the stable dry and wet fastness for the PET films, which could industriously expand the ink applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gelatin-based ink
Fluorinated waterborne polyurethane
Fluorocarbon quantum dots
Low surface energy
Anti-counterfeiting printing

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst misctext nomenclature orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The 1H NMR spectrum of FPO (a) and FP (b); The process of F-WPU dehydration and carbonization to form F-CDs (c). The FTIR of F-WPU (d), F-CDs (e) and GP (f).

SEM image of the surface (a1, b1, c1, d1, e1) and cross section (a2, b2, c2, d2, e2) of GA (a1, a2), GP3-0 (b1, b2), GP3-3 (c1, c2), GP3-4 (d1, d2) and GP3-5 (e1, e2). XRD of GP films (f). Schematic diagram of the distribution of gelatin, F-WPU and F-CDs in the composite (g).
The influence of F-WPU (a) and F-CDs (b) on the stress-strain curve of composite membranes.
The influence of F-WPU and F-CDs on the WCA of GP films (a) and inks (b).
The TGA (a) and DTG (b) curves of the composite membrane.

Fluorescence excitation-emission spectrum (a) and ultraviolet absorption spectrum (b) of 0.1 wt% F-CDs ethanol solution. Influence of mass fraction on the optimal excitation wavelength (c) and the fluorescence emission intensity under 410 nm wavelength irradiation (d) of F-CDs ethanol solution. Image of F-CDs ethanol solution under sunlight (e) and 365 nm ultraviolet light (f).
The fluorescence intensity changes of N-CDs in 0.1 wt% aqueous solution (a) and F-CDs in
0.1 wt% ethanol solution (b) before and after storage.

Ultraviolet–visible light transmittance spectrum (a) of the composite film. Images of composite emulsion (b) and composite film (c) under sunlight and 365 nm ultraviolet light. CIE chromaticity coordinates of ethanol solutions with different F-CDs content under 365 nm UV irradiation (d).
Images of different screen-printing patterns under sunlight (a1, b1, c1, d1) and 365 nm excitation wavelength (a2, b2, c2, d2).

The fastness test image of the printed pattern on the PET film: soaked in deionized water for 0 (a1), 24h (a2), 48h (a3) and 72h (a4). Rub with sandpaper 0 (b1), 20 (b2), 50 (b3) and 100 (b4) times. Varying degrees of curling, folding and violent crumpling (c1∼4). Changes in luminosity after treatment (d).
The ratio of components for different samples.
The elongation at break and tensile stress of different samples.
Water contact angle of composite films and printing patterns.
Color, light transmittance and transparency values of GP films.

head.ce:title: Environmentally friendly gelatin-based ink with durability on low surface energy substrates for anti-counterfeiting printing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Printing and Packaging, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430079, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . School of Printing and Packaging, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430079, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Marine Materials Corrosion and Protection, College of Materials, Xiamen University, Xiamen, 361000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Marine Materials Corrosion and Protection

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . Center for Marine Materials Corrosion and Protection, College of Materials, Xiamen University, Xiamen, 361000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

head.ce:miscellaneous: Handling Editor: Prof. Jiri Jaromir Klemeš

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recent research is keen to introduce biomass materials into inks to develop "green” printing, but little attention has been given to whether these inks can be truly widely used in printing industry. There are few bio-based inks suitable for low surface energy plastic such as polyethylene glycol terephthalate (PET), which is one of the most common printing substrates. Herein, waterborne polyurethane with polyfluoroalkyl side chain (F-WPU) was designed and synthesized to prepare fluorocarbon quantum dots (F-CDs). F-WPU and F-CDs were mixed together with gelatin to obtain environmentally friendly inks which were utilized to print on a low surface energy substrate. F-WPU (30 wt%) and F-CDs (0.5 wt%) significantly improve the mechanical properties of composites by forming an interpenetrating network system and hydrogen bonds, respectively. Abundant carbon-fluorine bonds effectively reduce the surface energy of gelatin, resulting in excellent hydrophobicity and strong adhesion to the PET surface. The thermal stability of the composite could significantly enhance to meet the promising needs for the further engineering applications. Besides, F-CDs could emit blue-green fluorescence based on conjugated structure and π→π* transition under a wider excitation wavelength. Therefore, the complex exhibited good transparency under sunlight and performed significant cyan fluorescence under an ultraviolet irradiation, in which can be efficiently developed and used in anti-counterfeiting labels and printing. Furthermore, the friction, soaking and kneading tests have been measured to seriously take the considerations for the stable dry and wet fastness for the PET films, which could industriously expand the ink applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are an inevitable by-product of traditional printing industry because of the widespread use of solvent-based inks (Fu et al., 2021; Sharma et al., 2021), which seriously polluted the atmosphere and harmed the health of workers. As the printing industry tends to be sustainable (Wu and Liao, 2021; Zhou et al., 2015), water-based ink has attracted more attention and biodegradable bio-based ink is more attractive in order to reduce waste (Baniasadi et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2018). Gelatin (GA) is a representative and sustainable natural polymer with abundant raw materials such as animal bones, skins, leather and other industrial waste (Curti et al., 2021; Dang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2021). In view of its excellent moldability and biocompatibility (Liu et al., 2021), GA is an ideal ink substrate and has been used in 3D printing and bio-inks(Song et al., 2020). However, GA-based inks are rarely reported in traditional printing, especially printing of plastic products, which are the mainstay of the printing industry (Horodytska et al., 2020; Sanchez-Caballero et al., 2021). This is because most plastic products have low surface energy to prevent getting dirty caused by the adhesion of dust, moisture and bacteria (Cai et al., 2019; Zolek-Tryznowska et al., 2020). However, low surface energy results in a weak interface between plastic and most natural polymers (Duan et al., 2017; Putnam and Wilker, 2021), resulting in poor adhesion of GA (Lindner et al., 2018; Putnam and Wilker, 2021). Since humidity and external force impact are not strictly controlled during the storage and transportation of general products, GA-based printing patterns are easily damaged due to strong water absorption and poor flexibility (Bigi et al., 2001; Ozbolat and Hospodiuk, 2016), and cannot replace solvent-based inks. In order to achieve a more extensive environmentally friendly printing, GA-based inks with low surface energy and good flexibility are of great significance.

Polymers and nanoparticles are usually incorporated into GA to adjust mechanical properties and surface energy (Liu et al., 2021; Roopavath et al., 2019). According to Gong et al. (2003), polymer composites could improve toughness by forming a double network structure, and nanoparticles can disperse stress to increase tensile strength (Curti et al., 2020). Low surface energy can be achieved by introducing hydrophobic groups (Gao et al., 2021). In order to meet the diverse industrial production, polymers/nanoparticles need to be able to be easily modified (Hospodiuk et al., 2017; Ji and Guvendiren, 2017). Many research activities have reported that polyurethanes and carbon quantum dots (CDs) were used to compound GA. Hsieh et al. (Hsieh and Hsu, 2019) developed a series of polyurethane-gelatin hydrogels with adjustable modulus as bio-inks. Lee et al. (2014) used gelatin and Waterborne polyurethane (WPU) from cold fish skin to prepare composite materials with enhanced mechanical properties and water resistance. Ghanbari et al. (2021) confirmed that CDs can simultaneously improve the flexibility and durability of GA and have safe biocompatibility. Wang et al. (2020) have used the CDs/GA composite system for rich fluorescence imaging. For polyurethanes, ideal mechanical properties can be achieved by changing the ratio of soft and hard segments and abundant functional groups can be built into the soft segments (Hsu et al., 2015; Yang et al., 2017). Owing to the presence of hydrophilic ionic groups, WPU can be stably dispersed in water and has been proven to be a clean binder for water-based inks (Liu et al., 2019). CDs is a new carbon-based particles and can be doped with various elements through one-pot hydrothermal treatment (Purbia and Paria, 2016), which is suitable for large-scale industrial production and does not produce VOCs (Schneider et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2022). It is well known that CDs has adjustable photoluminescence properties (Chen et al., 2014; Tan et al., 2021), which can contribute to the fluorescent anti-counterfeiting function of inks (Sun et al., 2017). Interestingly, WPU happens to be the raw material of CDs, and rare hydrophobic CDs can be prepared by increasing the content of hydrophobic groups in soft segment (Gu et al., 2016). Inspired by this, the triple compound of WPU, CDs and GA is worth considering. Among optional hydrophobic groups, fluorocarbon chains possess an extremely low surface free energy since fluorine develops the strongest electronegativity and lowest polarizability of all elements (Qu et al., 2009; Zhao et al., 2017). Polyfluoroalkyl was chosen because it is safe and low-toxic (Chinthakindi et al., 2021). Through hydrothermal treatment of F-WPU, fluorocarbon may be retained on the surface of CDs to reduce surface energy while improving fluorescence stability (Long et al., 2018). This is a cleaner process compared to traditional methods using hydrofluoric acid (Nebogatikova et al., 2014).

In order to obtain eco-friendly fluorescent anti-counterfeiting materials with hydrophobicity, polyurethane with polyfluoroalkyl side chain was synthesized, and F-CDs was directly fabricated by one-pot hydrothermal treatment of F-WPU. F-WPU and F-CDs were mixed together with GA to prepare composite films and inks. As a typical low surface energy plastic (Fernandez-Menendez et al., 2020), PET was selected as the printing substrate. The molecular structure, optical properties, surface contact angles, mechanical properties, thermodynamic properties and printability of the composite films and inks were comprehensively characterized. The excellent anti-counterfeiting practical value has been confirmed. As far as we know, there has never been a study on fluorinated CDs directly from polyurethane and achieve homogeneous triple complexation of GA-WPU-CDs, which provides a new potential scheme for novel fluorescent anti-counterfeiting materials.

2-(2,2,3,3-Tetrafluoro-propoxymethyl)-oxirane (FPO) was synthesized by the reported study (Zhang et al., 2021a,b). Boron trifluoride diethyl ether (BF3·OEt2) was purchased from J&K scientific Ltd. Isophorone diisocyanate (IPDI), gelatin (GA, CP), glycerin (GL) and 2,2-Bis(hydroxymethyl) propionic acid (DMPA) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. 1,4-butanediol (BDO), triethylamine (TEA), dibutyltin dilaurate (DBTDL), acetone and dichloromethane were available from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. FPO was dried under low pressure at 85 °C for 3 h before use. The compound defoamer was obtained from Henan Midaner Trading Co., Ltd. The methylene chloride was distilled over CaH2. Acetone was stored with 4 Å type molecular sieves for further use. Other reagents and solvents were utilized without any purification. Screen printing plates and PET films were purchased in local suppliers. Deionized water was used throughout the experiment.

BDO (5.00 mmol, 0.45 g) was dissolved in CH2Cl2 after being vacuum distilled. Then BF3·OEt2 (11.50 mmol, 1.69 g) was added and magnetically stirred in below 0 °C for 30 min in a nitrogen atmosphere (Zhang et al., 2021a,b). Then, FPO (77.60 mmol, 14.55 g) was slowly dropped into the system within 6 h, and the reaction was continuously stirred for 12 h. Finally, deionized water (5 ml) was added to cap the hydroxyl group of the prepolymer. The reaction mixture was washed with 10 wt% NaCl aqueous solution, and further vacuum distilled to obtain fluorine-containing polyether (FP, Mn = 2569 g/moL).

The dried FP (3.00 mmol, 7.71 g) and IPDI (9.00 mmol, 2.00 g) were added into a three-neck round bottom flask for heating at 60 °C and stirred under nitrogen atmosphere. After sufficient magnetic stirring, the temperature was raised to 85 °C for other 2 h reaction. Then DMPA (6.00 mmol, 0.80g) and DBTDL (as a catalyst for 1 wt% of total mass) were added to the system and continued to react for 2 h. Acetone was used to reduce the viscosity of the reaction system. Finally, the mixture was cooled down to 30 °C, and TEA (6.60 mmol, 0.67 g) was reacted with neutralize carboxyl group of DMPA for 30 min. Then, deionized water (44.72 g) was used as dispersant and mechanically stirred (1400 r/min, 15 min) to obtain a translucent and uniform F-WPU emulsion with a solid content of 20 wt%. Acetone was recovered by rotary evaporation.

F-CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method. The F-WPU emulsion was placed in autoclaves and heated at 250 °C for 24 h to obtain brown liquid. The particulate matter was removed by filtration and centrifugation. The supernatant was chromatographed with ethyl acetate and dried into a yellow-brown powder insoluble in water, and it was dissolved in absolute ethanol to obtain a 1 wt% solution for further use.

GP films and ink were prepared respectively for further characterization. For films, GA was dissolved in deionized water and heated at 55 °C to obtain a 5 wt% solution. After that, GA solution, F-WPU emulsion, F-CDs solution and GL (as plasticizer, 10 wt% of GA) were stirred and mixed according to different formulas (Table 1 ). Finally, the mixture was placed in the 55mm × 55 mm glass mold and heated at 35 °C in an oven for 12 h to obtain GP films. For ink, GA was dissolved in deionized water at 40 °C to obtain a solution with a solid content of 50 wt%. Compound defoamer (0.2 wt%) and glycerin (30 wt%) were added and fully magnetically stirred. Finally, F-WPU and F-CDs were added according to the formula in Table 1 to obtain viscous ink at room temperature (Curti et al., 2021).

1H NMR spectra measurements were recorded on Mercury VX-300 spectrometer. FPO and FP were respectively dissolved in CDCl3 containing tetramethylsilane as internal standard and packed in the nuclear magnetic tubes. Fourier transform-infrared spectra (FTIR) of F-WPU, F-CDs and GP was obtained on a Nicolet 5700 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Electron Corporation, Waltham, MA). The spectral range was from 4000 to 500 cm−1.

The morphology of GP was performed with scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi FE-SEM 4800, Japan). The films were sprayed with gold for 1 min. The surface morphology and cross-section were characterized under high vacuum mode at 15 kV. Images were captured at 5000 × magnification after GP films were fractured by liquid nitrogen.

The thermal stability was performed on a thermogravimetric measurement (TG209 F1 Libra thermobalance). GP films samples (5–10 mg) were heated from 30 to 600 °C (10 °C/min) under a nitrogen atmosphere (20 mL/min).

Thickness of GP films was measured at five different positions with a digital micrometer (Model IP65, SAMA Tools, Viareggio, Italy). The average of these five values was recorded. The mechanical property was performed with a universal tensile testing machine (Instron 3343, USA) at a crosshead speed of 50 mm/min. GP films were cut into 25mm × 5 mm standard samples. The final results were recorded as the average of three measurements.

WCA of GP films and printing patterns was recorded with a video optical contact angle measuring instrument (OCA15EC, Germany Defi Instrument Co., Ltd.) with sessile method. GP films/PET films with printed patterns were fixed on a smooth metal base and deionized water (4 μL) was dropped slowly on the surface of films. WCA of each sample was measured after 1minture.

The transparency of GP films was tested with a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Ultrospec, 2000; Scinteck, UK). The light transmittance was measured by scanning films at wavelengths from 200 to 800 nm. The fluorescence spectra of F-CDs solution were collected on an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Images of GP films, inks and printed patterns were collected under sunlight and 365 nm UV lamp irradiation. The color difference of GP films was performed on a colorimeter (Ultra Scan Pro, HunterLab, USA). GP films were stacked on the white standard plate, and the parameters (L*, a*, b*) were measured according to the literature method. The films were measured and recorded on the surface at least three different positions for the average value. The formula to calculate the color difference was shown in Eq. (1):
Eq. (1)
Δ
E ∗
=
( Δ
L ∗
)
2
+
( Δ
a ∗
)
2
+
( Δ
b ∗
)
2
ΔE* is color difference. ΔL*, Δa* and Δb* are respectively the color difference between films and white standard plate (L* = 95.11, a* = 2.27, and b* = −12.86).

The 1H NMR spectrum of FPO and FP were analyzed in Fig. 1 (a) and Fig. 1b respectively. For FPO, the peaks during 5.74–6.04 ppm were assigned to –CF2H and the peak of –CH2 in –OCH2CF2- appeared at 3.91 ppm, which proves that 2,2,3,3-tetrafluoro-1-propanol has successfully replaced -Cl in epichlorohydrin. According to the peaks appearing at 3.12 ppm (e, –CH), 2.58 ppm and 2.78 ppm (d and d’, –CH2), the epoxy group was confirmed to be intact, which is the structural basis of the subsequent cationic ring-opening polymerization reaction (Zhang et al., 2021a,b). For FP, the peaks during 5.71–6.01 ppm were the character of –CF2H, which confirmed the introduction of polyfluoroalkyl side chains. The peaks around 1.21 ppm were attributed to –OCH2CH2CH2CH2O- and proved successful chain extension (Q. Zhang et al., 2019).

The FTIR of F-WPU were observed in Fig. 2 (d). The absorption band at 1234 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of –CF2-. The characteristic absorption bands of polyurethane were observed at 1541 cm−1 (C–N bonds and δ N–H bonds), 1650 cm−1 (-CO stretching vibration) and 1104 cm−1 (–COC– stretching vibration). The peaks around 1457, 2871 and 2923 cm−1 were the characteristics of –CH2-, corresponding to the in-plane bending vibration, symmetrical and antisymmetrical stretching vibration of C–H, respectively (Qu et al., 2014). The absorption wavelength of –OH locates around 3287 cm−1. The wider and flat band means that –OH tends to be intramolecular associative state rather than free state, which implied the hydrogen bonds formed in F-WPU. Overall, the results of FTIR indicated that F-WPU had been prepared successfully. For F-CDs, the absorption bands of –CF2- (1259 cm−1), –COC– (1100 cm−1) and –CH2- (2921, 2853, and 1458 cm−1) were still retained (Fig. 2e). Compared with F-WPU, sharper alkyl absorption peaks and weakened –CO absorption peaks symbolized more hydrophobic groups and damaged hydrophilic groups such as –COOH (Fig. 2c). The new peak appeared near 1604 cm−1 was the characteristic region of –CC- and low absorption wavelength was the result of the conjugated system, which means that sp2 hybrid orbitals were formed in F-CDs (Gu et al., 2016). Fig. 2 (f) showed the FTIR of GP films. The absorption peaks of GP3-0 around 1100 cm−1 were obviously strengthened, which proved that C–F bond was introduced. The wide absorption band of GP complex that was retained around 3287 cm−1, which means the –OH on F-CDs could associate with GA and F-WPU through hydrogen bonds.

The microscopic distribution of films was analyzed with SEM by observing the surface and cross-sectional state. Fig. 3 (a2) showed the obvious light and dark alternation on the cross section of the GA film, which was caused by the stacking of GA. After F-WPU was mixed in, the surface of GP3-0 film was still smooth while the alternating light and dark on the cross section got tighter. When the content of F-CDs is 0.5 wt%, the phenomenon of alternating light and dark on the cross section has disappeared, implying that the compatibility of F-WPU soft and hard segments is enhanced. The reason is that the hydroxyl groups on the surface of F-CDs could form hydrogen bonds with carbonyl groups and –NH–, which are widely found in F-WPU and gelatin (Fig. 3g) (Qiang et al., 2020). The uniform morphology means that F-CDs can be evenly distributed in the matrix and make the interpenetrating cross-linked network system closer. However, with the further addition of F-CDs, the soft and hard segments of polyurethane separate again (Fig. 3d2) and tend to be chaotic due to the limited space for molecular motion (Fig. 3e2), results in damaged morphology (Fig. 3e1) and uneven mechanical properties.

The crystal structure of GP films was further characterized through XRD (Fig. 3 (f)). GA showed peaks of triple-helix crystalline structures and amorphous fractions at 19.4° (2θ) (Liu et al., 2017). GP3-0 showed flatter peak values, which meant the destruction of intramolecular association and the decrease of crystallinity and proved the existence of the double network structure (Ahammed et al., 2020). The peak was restored after the addition of F-CDs and a sharp peak appeared near 19.5° (2θ) at a content of 0.5 wt%. Reappearing peaks came from the separation of the soft and hard segments of F-WPU and is consistent with the SEM results.

Necessary toughness can prevent the printed pattern from being damaged by external forces, which is the basis for the application of gelatin-based inks. Mechanical properties of GP films with different proportions are given in Fig. 4 and Table 2 . The elongation at break and the breaking stress of GP films were increased after adding F-WPU, and reached the maximum at a content of 30 wt%. This shows that the interpenetrating cross-linked network system was formed between GA and F-WPU, and the hydrogen bonds between molecular chains also enhance the mechanical properties (Ahammed et al., 2020). However, as the content of WPU further increased, the overall crystallinity of the films got worse, resulting in a decrease in rigidity and tensile strength. Further, an obvious enhancement of mechanical properties of GP nanocomposites were observed at low loading of F-CDs, which was attributed to the uniform dispersion of F-CDs in GA/F-WPU. Since –OH existed on the surface of nanoparticles, F-CDs could form hydrogen bonds with both gelatin and F-WPU. This interaction made the molecular chains tightly packed, which increases the tensile stress and elongation at break (Ezati et al., 2022). However, higher contents of F-CDs resulted in agglomeration and less compatibility due to the limited interstitial space of the GA/F-WPU interpenetrating network, which can be proved from Fig. 4 (b) since 0.5 wt% F-CDs could optimize the mechanical properties of GP films. Interestingly, this ratio is consistent with the maximum fluorescence intensity.

Poor thermal stability is a disadvantage of most bio-based inks. Thermal stability plays an important role in the production, storage and application of polymers. The thermal stability of GP was analyzed according to TG (Fig. 6 a) and DTG (Fig. 6b). The thermal decomposition process of GA could be divided into three stages. The weight loss at 100 °C was attributed to the evaporation of water remaining in the composite membrane. The significant weight loss around 260 °C and 320 °C is attributed to the thermal degradation of glycerin and gelatin. After F-WPU and F-CDs were added, the weight loss peak at 320 °C was retained while the peak near 260 °C got inconspicuous. Weight loss occurred around 425 °C was caused by the decomposition of main chain and fluorine-containing side chains in F-WPU. The change in the thermal decomposition process means F-WPU and gelatin form a dense cross-linking system, which improves the thermal stability of the polymer. In addition, the reduction in weight loss at 100 °C proves the increased hydrophobicity. Overall, the temperature for 50% weight loss increased from 316 °C to about 353 °C. Excellent thermal stability allows gelatin-based inks to be used in a wider temperature range.

The yellowish dispersion of F-CDs under daylight exhibited blue-green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light irradiation (Fig. 7 a, e, f). A strong absorption band at 273 nm was observed, which was ascribed to the π→π∗ transition of –CC–CC- (Fig. 7b)(Gu et al., 2016). Multiple bonds were formed by intra- and intermolecular dehydration during hydrothermal treatment. Conjugated system explained the deeper color of high concentration F-CDs and the red shift of fluorescence excitation spectrum. Owing to the π-π conjugation effect, delocalized π bonds were formed and the electrons are easily excited. So, the absorption peak wavelength increases, and the chromogenic effect was strengthened. In the range of 0.01–1.00 wt%, the optimal excitation wavelength of F-CDs increased from 390 nm to 430 nm (Fig. 7c). But the bright fluorescence under 365 nm UV means F-CDs had a wider excitation spectrum, which was assigned to the n→π∗ and π→π∗ transitions of sub-fluorophores (e.g., C–N/CN, and C–F) (Liu et al., 2021). The optimal emission wavelength is obtained around 498 nm and displayed excitation independent PL emission. The photoluminescence intensity reached the highest around 0.1 wt% (Fig. 7d). F-CDs with π conjugation had π-π interactions, leading to π-π stacking and fluorescence quenching with increasing concentration, which is a typical aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) progress (Yan et al., 2021).

In addition, C–F shows good resistance to UV degradation although it has not been found to participate in fluorescence emission. After 30 days of storage at room temperature in sunlight, CDs synthesized from urea and citric acid (N-CDs) were visibly weakened (Shen et al., 2019). In contrast, the fluorescence of F-CDs remained basically unchanged (Fig. 8 ). The decrease in fluorescence intensity can be attributed to the oxidation of N-CDs at room temperature. The semi-ionic C--F bonds can enhance intramolecular and intermolecular hydrogen bonding and reduce the quenching of fluorescence without the oxygen barrier (Long et al., 2018). C–F improves the stability of CDs, which means longer product life and less raw material consumption.

It is easy to understand that the increased concentration will trigger ACQ and reduce the fluorescence intensity. However, simultaneous red shift of emission spectrum implied the existence of AIE. On the one hand, dispersion in the polymer matrix could prevent direct contact between F-CDs. On the other hand, an unusual fluorescence color change thanks to elevated concentration was observed since functionalities at the edges and introduction of defects restricted π-π molecular interactions, which means AIE and ACQ coexisted during F-CDs (Y. Zhang et al., 2019). In tighter solid dispersion system, F-CDs tend to be stacked in a head-to-tail J -aggregates rather than a face-to-face H-aggregates. For F-CDs, J aggregation was induced by molecular aggregation and resulted in red shift of emission spectra (Ozyurt et al., 2021). As shown in Fig. 9d, the fluorescence of GP films shifted from blue to cyan in CIE color space with the increase of F-CDs. In the aggregate state, due to the restriction of molecular motion, F-CDs formed large conjugated clusters, reduced the non-radiative decay and facilitated AIE effect (Long et al., 2017).

To examine the practicability of GP ink, customized patterns like quick response code (QR code), block bamboo pattern, text and linear cat pattern were deposited onto surfaces of PET films through simple screen printing. These patterns were imperceptible under sunlight. In the case of mirror emission, these patterns are translucent like watermark, which is already a basic anti-counterfeiting effect. The good printability of the ink on the PET surface could be verified. Patterns could emit strong blue-green fluorescence and clearly reproduce the complete printed information under 365 nm UV illumination, which is a dual anti-counterfeiting based on wide excitation band (Fig. 10 ). The excellent dispersibility of F-CDs in GA and F-WPU is further confirmed by the uniform fluorescent effect, especially the clear fine line pattern. Upon turning off the UV lamp, fluorescence disappears immediately, showing necessary hidden performance for secret anti-counterfeiting mark. In addition, the hidden information in QR codes can be read out easily with smartphone, thus realizing double anti-counterfeiting protection.

To examine fastness of GP ink, the fluorescent intensity of the select pattern screen-printed on PET film were evaluated after being soaked, rubbed and folded. Lab model was used to compare the color changes of patterns. The impressive wet fastness of ink is shown in Fig. 11 (a1∼a4). There was almost no damage to the pattern after being soaked in water for 24 h. The luminosity drop of fluorescent color was still within 3.5 even after 72h (Fig. 11d), which was attributed to the hydrophobic groups of F-WPU and F-CDs. Fluorine group created excellent adhesion of ink on the PET surface, no matter in dry or wet environment. The resistance of ink to friction was relatively weak. Cotton cloth, kraft paper and sandpaper were all used to rub patterns and the former two have almost no effect on fluorescence. However, obvious defects as shown in Fig. 11. (b2∼b4) appeared after being rubbed by sandpaper for 20, 50, and 100 times. The low ink hardness was caused by the softness of gelatin and F-WPU in amorphous state. Despite the sharp drop in maximum fluorescence (Fig. 11d), the underlying ink could still be preserved intact as a result of the outstanding adhesion fastness, without affecting the anti-counterfeiting function. Fig. 11(a1∼a4) exhibits the pattern of bamboo on the surfaces of PET film was still discernible under 365 nm irradiation after being curled, folded and violently crumpled. Fluorescent effect fluctuated due to the decrease in surface flatness, but the magnitude of luminosity change was less than 1/10 (Fig. 11d). F-WPU contributed to the good flexibility of ink to withstand severe deformation. Collectively, the excellent dry and wet fastness demonstrates that F-WPU and F-CDs endows GA ink with universal practicability.

At present, researchers are committed to promoting the degradable water-based inks to eliminate serious pollution in the traditional printing industry, however, the poor stability of the natural polymers on many common low surface energy printing substrates limits the application ranges of biomass inks. This research proposes an environmentally friendly gelatin-based anti-counterfeiting ink. The combination of F-WPU and F-CDs can contribute to the outstanding printing performance of gelatin-based water-based inks on PET surfaces. F-WPU and GA form an interpenetrating cross-linked network system to reduce the crystallinity of gelatin, while F-CDs further improve the flexibility through hydrogen bonding, resulting in the best mechanical properties at 30 wt% F-WPU and 0.5 wt % F-CDs, respectively. The hydrophobicity of GP gradually enhanced with the increased fluorine content. F-CDs showed the obvious hydrophobicity relative to the ordinary CDs, and efficiently improve the stability of GA in humid environments. Besides, the thermal stability of GA was also significantly improved. More importantly, the photoluminescence properties of F-CDs contributed to perform the anti-counterfeiting properties due to the wide excitation band and the stable fluorescence. This research provides a feasible strategy for the wider application of bio-based inks in printing industry.
This work was supported by the Nature Science Associate Foundation (NSAF) of China (Grant numbers: U1830127).

Wenshuo Zhang: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Tao Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Formal analysis. Yajun Deng: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Zhihao Guan: Data curation, Software. Yuye Zhong: Investigation, Validation. Shuliang Fang: Validation. Guannan Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xinghai Liu: Resources, Formal analysis. Houbin Li: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
